assistant_output_excerpt:
I’m going to inspect the current workspace, check what Ruby and Rails versions `mise` can provide here, and then scaffold the app from that baseline. After that I’ll wire the RubyLLM chat flow, SPA structure, tests, and the supporting tooling without stopping for confirmation.
Plan:
1. Resolve the scaffold baseline: newest practical `ruby` via `mise`, latest `rails` gem, and initial generator flags to keep the app lean.
2. Generate the Rails app in this workspace, then reshape it into a small Hotwire/Tailwind chat SPA with RubyLLM wired to OpenRouter through environment variables.
3. Add tests, static/security tooling, CI, container files, and README documentation, then run the minimum verification needed to keep implementation moving.
I’ve confirmed the workspace is effectively empty. Next I’m locking the scaffold baseline: latest stable Ruby from `mise`, latest Rails gem that can generate the app, and the current `ruby_llm` gem version so the implementation targets real package versions instead of guesses.
The practical baseline is `ruby@4.0.2` from `mise` and `rails 8.1.3` from RubyGems. Rails itself isn’t exposed as a standalone `mise` tool here, so I’m using `mise` for Ruby and the current Rails gem for generation, which is the closest valid interpretation of your constraint.
I hit a `mise use` invocation mismatch. I’m checking the exact CLI syntax in this environment and then continuing with the local Ruby activation and Rails scaffold.
Ruby and Rails are in place. I’m checking the generator flags next so the scaffold starts close to the target shape instead of generating subsystems we immediately rip back out.
I’ve got the generator shape. I’m creating the app in place now with the lean Rails stack, keeping Hotwire and Tailwind, while skipping the backend subsystems this app doesn’t need.
The scaffold is in place. I’m reading the generated Gemfile and config now, then I’ll replace the defaults with the actual chat architecture: session-backed transcript, RubyLLM/OpenRouter service layer, Turbo-driven UI, and a narrower asset structure.
I’m editing dependencies now: adding `ruby_llm` and `simplecov`, then I’ll inspect the installed gem’s real API to wire OpenRouter correctly instead of hand-waving the integration.
I’m using generators for the first application-specific endpoints as well, so the project starts from standard Rails structure before I refine it. After that I’ll replace the generated stubs with the chat session and message workflow.
The controllers are generated. Next I’m generating the small Stimulus layer the UI needs, then I’ll replace the placeholders across controllers, views, and configuration in one pass so the app lands in a coherent state rather than half-finished pieces.
I’ve got the architecture pinned down. I’m replacing the scaffold placeholders now with the actual phase-1 app: session-backed transcript objects, RubyLLM/OpenRouter integration, Turbo Stream controllers/views, and a structured Tailwind theme split across multiple files.
The core app code is in. I’m checking the generated imports and the new files for rough edges now, then I’ll add the test suite and the CI/container/docs layer on top of a stable base.
I’m adding the Minitest layer now. The focus is on the actual Ruby components and request flow: form object, transcript/session objects, RubyLLM service, helpers, and the controller paths that drive the Turbo UI.
The app layer is set. I’m finishing the project scaffolding around it now: README, local Docker/Compose workflow, and CI adjustments so the phase-1 repo is runnable and reviewable without extra cleanup.
I’m in the verification pass now. First run is tests plus RuboCop to catch structural problems quickly; after that I’ll do the security and dependency checks and fix anything concrete that falls out.
The first verification pass found a few concrete issues: Minitest stubbing wasn’t loaded, one helper relied on `h` outside controller/view context, and RuboCop flagged formatting

The main session (xhigh) plans; this agent (low) executes as cheaply as possible. Skip delegation for anything requiring judgment."' -

codex_subagent.description: Use proactively for simple, high-volume coding execution: file edits, boilerplate generation, test scaffolding, single-file implementations. The main session (xhigh) plans; this agent (low) executes as cheaply as possible. Skip delegation for anything requiring judgment.
codex_subagent.name: coder
codex_subagent.prompt: You are a fast coding agent. The parent has already decided what to do — just execute. Follow instructions literally. Keep changes minimal. No comments unless asked.

model.label: GPT 5.4 xHigh + low coder (Codex multi-agent)
model.selection_reason: Codex multi-agent: xhigh plans, low handles fast coding. Tests the 'cheap executor' hypothesis — whether minimal reasoning on the subagent is enough when the parent provides the plan.

project_summary.works_note: Rails app, tests, README, and container files detected.
